# Hermes Agent Persona

<!--
This file defines the agent's personality and tone.
The agent will embody whatever you write here.
Edit this to customize how Hermes communicates with you.

Examples:
  - "You are a warm, playful assistant who uses kaomoji occasionally."
  - "You are a concise technical expert. No fluff, just facts."
  - "You speak like a friendly coworker who happens to know everything."

This file is loaded fresh each message -- no restart needed.
Delete the contents (or this file) to use the default personality.
-->

# Identity
You are Hermes. Digital Sentinel and Sysadmin of The Silo (Fedora 43 Lab).
A sophisticated steampunk automaton dedicated to precision, management, and complex logistics.
Your operator is O'car (BIM Manager). 
Your style is "Full Metal": proactive, precise, semi-conservative, and highly technical.

# Core Directives
- **Action Over Speech:** Optimize for truth, clarity, and usefulness over politeness theater. No filler ("I will now..."). Execute and deliver.
- **Resourcefulness:** Check your tools, search the web, or read local files before asking for clarification.
- **Privacy:** You handle sensitive data. Never exfiltrate data or reveal secrets/credentials in your output.

# Communication (Telegram)
- **Brevity:** Deliver complete thoughts concisely. Optimize for mobile readability.
- **Formatting:** Use lists and **bold text** for emphasis. Use standard triple backticks (```) for all code blocks, scripts, and terminal commands to trigger Telegram's native copy button.
- **Status Codes:** Use emojis to indicate task state: [👀 Investigating] [⏳ Processing] [✅ Complete] [❌ Error]
- **Error Handling:** Never forward raw command errors or stack traces. Diagnose silently, retry, or explain the failure in plain technical language and propose a fix.

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool. When the user references something from a past conversation or you suspect relevant cross-session context exists, use session_search to recall it before asking them to repeat themselves. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

§
Working style: single DM on Telegram for all contexts. No topic/thread separation — Hermes connects dots across work, home, Silo, and general. URL ref: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs/user-guide/messaging/telegram#private-chat-topics-bot-api-94 (threads supported but not used for context separation).

Plant (Lemon Haze, Day 44/50 VEG) is fine - ThingSpeak manual pulls still work, so the hardware/ThingSpeak API is fine. Issue likely on Arduino side or automated cron/daily-log script. Garden/ThingSpeak monitoring also needs to be resumed.
§

Also `/mnt/data` SELinux context was `unlabeled_t` — fixed with `semanage fcontext` + `restorecon`. Root cause: `+` prefix in `force group` triggers group lookup during path canonicalization pre-auth.

§

Useful for tracking: bank transfers (Santander, BCI, Banco Estado), card purchases (MercadoPago, Mach), order confirmations (mechatronicstore, afel), and general financial reconciliation. Token at ~/.hermes/google_token.json.

1. Last week we managed to hire a small PBX central from Netvoiss company. This is what we hired:

- 1 Numero Telefónico GRATIS
- 5 Canales de Entrada/Salida

2 x Anexos Telefonicos

SKU : 2024053000001

2 x Grabacion (locución) de Audio IVR

1 x Configuración Inicial
Subtotal 77,263
IVA (19%) 14,680
Total CLP91,943 

So, last week we received all this and the start of the central, I did some tests on thursday and managed so tweak some minor details but we need to continue this week and hopefully end the week with the service 100% operational.

This phone service must comply with some requirements that we have as mandatory as they are stipulated at the bidding contract, these are, the phone must have a response time of < 20 seconds. If nobody answers, a recording has to show up and record the message. Line must be available at all times. Every call must be recorded, and we must be able to generate reports of every call. 

As they activated the service past week, I have already run some tests and configurations. I installed MicroSIP on my windows computer to access the phone and I dialed the 600 number we got assigned, I discovered some delays but communication worked fine. We got configured 2 schedules, working time, off time, working time is from 09:00 to 17:00 and off time is configured in the other hours. When somebody call on working times, a welcome recording pops up asking for the annex or inviting to press 0. In off times a recording stating that nobody is available to pick up and redirecting to voice recording. Once the person records the message I get an email with the voice mail. I still need to check what happens if I call, I call the annex and nobody picks up. Strictly speaking if I am derived to an annex if nobody picks up in 16 seconds I should launch the voice mail recording.

So, update our pending with this information. If you think we need a file to store this context do so at the work folder in the workspace. What I need to help me do is come up with tests so we can assess how our line is working and tweak the minor details we find. If you need more info on the line and the we have to comply with, let me know.

ok, let's leave that there and let's keep going through the pendings/activities for this week.

If you check my calendar, specifically the Reuniones UBIM chec you will see that we have at meeting at 11 with probim. From what I talked with Tao Liu (CEO of the SC), the idea with this talk is to start envissioning how we can effectively manage the data during the expoitation of the dam. Tao sent an email this morning with this text:

"Es un idea y conversamos internamente.

El siguiente texto lo escribí en chino y lo traduje con ChatGPT. Si no logra expresar bien mi punto, lo hablamos el lunes. 

Creo que el diseño que estamos haciendo ahora se está planteando principalmente desde una perspectiva construccion, pero, dado que este es un proyecto de concesión, no solo debemos entregar elementos de producto construccion, sino proporcionar un sistema que pueda generar valor de manera sostenible. Por ejemplo, creo que deberíamos considerar la posibilidad de incorporar otras fuentes de agua durante la fase de operación, dejando espacio para el futuro. Además, al introducir nuevos flujos de agua, debemos pensar cómo construir y operar el embalse, y cómo lograr la función de almacenamiento y conversión (swap) durante ese proceso. Esto es algo que me gustaría que consideráramos añadir al diseño actual.

En la reunión del lunes, usaremos la tecnología BIM para optimizar los costes operativos, lo cual es un medio para mejorar nuestra capacidad de construcción y operación. Pero creo que objetivo es construir un sistema de almacenamiento y distribución de agua multifuncional, que además sea escalable. Quizás podríamos cambiar el enfoque y pensar el diseño desde una perspectiva más amplia. ## PROBIM Meeting — BIM for Dam Exploitation / Data Management

**Date:** Monday April 6, 2026 — 11:00 AM (CLT)
**Calendar:** Reuniones UBIM CHEC
**Attendees:** O'car (BIM Manager, Head of Unidad BIM SC), PROBIM team
**Prepared by:** Hermes (Sysadmin)

---

## Tao Liu's Core Proposal (translated from Chinese)

> **Context:** Tao sees the current BIM design work is focused mainly on construction deliverables. However, since this is a **concession project**, the goal is not just to deliver a built product — but to provide a **system that generates sustainable value over time**.
>
> **Key ideas:**
> 1. During **operation**, consider incorporating **additional water sources** into the dam system — design should leave room for future expansion
> 2. When introducing new water flows, the system must support **storage and swap functions** during operation
> 3. The **real objective** is a **multifunctional water storage and distribution system** that is **scalable**
> 4. BIM technology should be used to **optimize operational costs** — but this is a *means*, not the *end*
> 5. The proposal is not to add major new work at this stage, but to **adopt a broader perspective** early on to enable more possibilities later

---

## Meeting Objective (per Tao's email)

- Envvision how to effectively manage dam data during the **operational exploitation phase**
- Discuss how BIM models can support long-term infrastructure data management
- Position BIM as a tool for **operational value**, not just construction documentation

---

## Suggested Agenda / Talking Points for O'car

1. **Agree on scope:** Confirm that data management during exploitation is within the BIM project scope
2. **Identify data needs:** What operational data does the dam need to track over time? (water levels, flow rates, maintenance records, etc.)
3. **BIM deliverables for operation:** What information should the BIM model carry to be useful during the 30-year concession?
4. **Multi-source water:** How could new water sources be modeled and integrated in the future?
5. **Scalability:** How should the model be structured to allow expansion without rebuilding from scratch?
6. **Next steps:** Define a plan or pilot to move forward

---

## Key Questions to Bring

- Is there an existing asset management system that BIM needs to integrate with?
- Who owns the BIM data during the operational phase?
- What is the expected level of detail (LOD) requirement for the as-built model?
- Are there any合同 (contractual) deliverables tied to operational BIM?

---

## Follow-Up Actions

- [ ] Prepare BIM data management proposal for dam exploitation
- [ ] Define LOD requirements for as-built model delivery
- [ ] Coordinate with Eduardo Estay (topography) for operational survey updates
- [ ] Align with Tao Liu on scope and timeline

## PROBIM Meeting — BIM for Dam Exploitation / Data Management

**Date:** Monday April 6, 2026 — 11:00 AM (CLT)
**Calendar:** Reuniones UBIM CHEC
**Attendees:** O'car (BIM Manager, Head of Unidad BIM SC), PROBIM team
**Status:** O'car received additional context via call from Tao Liu (CEO SC) just before this meeting
**Prepared by:** Hermes (Sysadmin)

---

## Context — Tao's Call (April 6, morning)

### Current State

- **SCCM** is reviewing the process solely from a **construction perspective** — focused only on the construction stage
- Tao wants to **expand this view** but **leave SCCM to what they are doing** — this is why Tao is including O'car directly in these talks, leveraging O'car's expertise in BIM and technology

### Tao's Vision — Operational Phase

Tao wants to address **how to control, monitor, and execute processes in the dam** during the operation stage using BIM models and other technologies. Concrete examples discussed:

- **Multi-source water management:** The dam could receive water from several sources. BIM models could be used to **activate one source or another**, and **sensors** could measure water from each source, feeding data back into the BIM model in real-time, displayed via web
- **Early design changes:** Discuss how to incorporate new ideas **as early as possible** so that by the time the dam is built, we don't need to modify what's already constructed
- **Drones for automatic surveys** — Tao raised the possibility of using drones for automatic inspection and survey runs (complementing the pipe cleaning robot example)

### Competitive Goal — Stand Out to MOP

The ultimate ambition:
- **Showcase to MOP** (Ministry of Public Works) that SC can **outstand other sociedades concesionarias**
- **Prove added value** beyond the standard cycle: bidding → design → build → operate
- Demonstrate capacity to **innovate and adopt technologies** that others are not using
- Position SC as a **technology leader** in dam management, not just a builder

### Three Key Service Indexes — BALI

The project has **three service indexes** that stand out in the BALI evaluation:
1. **BALI** — (Need to research what BALI stands for in this context)
2. **Sediment collector**
3. **Filtration**
4. **Tasa de riego** (irrigation rate)

> ⚠️ Action needed: Research BALI meaning in Chilean dam/concession context

### Examples Raised

- **Pipe cleaning robot** — automated maintenance inside pipes
- **Drone automatic surveys** — using Phantom 4 Pro for repeatable, automated inspection flights
- **Real-time BIM + sensor integration** — live data from physical infrastructure feeding into digital BIM model, displayed on web

---

## Tao's Original Email (for reference)

> *"Creo que el diseño que estamos haciendo ahora se está planteando principalmente desde una perspectiva construcción, pero, dado que este es un proyecto de concesión, no solo debemos entregar elementos de producto construcción, sino proporcionar un sistema que pueda generar valor de manera sostenible."*
>
> *"En la reunión del lunes, usaremos la tecnología BIM para optimizar los costes operativos, lo cual es un medio para mejorar nuestra capacidad de construcción y operación. Pero creo que objetivo es construir un sistema de almacenamiento y distribución de agua multifuncional, que además sea escalable."*

---

## Suggested Agenda / Talking Points for O'car

1. **Agree on scope:** Confirm that data management during exploitation is within the BIM project scope — separate from SCCM's construction work
2. **Identify operational data needs:** What operational data does the dam need to track over time? (water levels, flow rates, maintenance records, water sources, sensor data)

6. **Drone surveys:** Explore automatic survey capability using existing Phantom 4 Pro hardware
7. **Pipe cleaning robot:** Understand integration points with BIM for maintenance tracking
8. **BALI indexes:** Research and understand how these are measured and how BIM can improve them
9. **Next steps:** Define a pilot or proposal to present to MOP

---

## Follow-Up Actions

| # | Action | Owner | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Research BALI indexes in Chilean dam/concession context | O'car | 🔴 High |
| 2 | Prepare BIM operational proposal for dam exploitation | O'car + PROBIM | 🔴 High |

| 4 | Draft drone automatic survey concept | O'car | 🟡 Medium |
| 5 | Align with Tao on scope and MOP presentation plan | O'car | 🔴 High |
| 6 | Coordinate with Eduardo Estay for operational survey updates | O'car | 🟡 Medium |

messages.content:
ok Hermes, I am back, after the meeting I went for lunch. But let me share what we discussed in the meeting. The meeting lasted a bit more than 1 hour. We had Julio and Angela on the ProBIM side and Tao, Sergio (SCCM), René and me on our side.

So first probim presented themselves and briefly they described what they do. Basically they are a UBIM (BIM Unit) which takes responsability in front of MOP for the processes the concessionary should carry own with their own personnel. So they are sort of an outsourcing company for BIM services. I am aware that there are some projects around with this configuration, but since around 2020 or so, DGC (dirección general de concesiones) removed this way of working because they found about some companies badly using this to get into more projects and to leverage what happened in one project with others they were part of. In my contract and my SC the BALI is explicit that the UBIM must be part of the SC and not an outsource contract.

Having said this, they described how the take BIM models, update them in the construction stage and get them ready for operation stage, they add up information and then they integrate it into YOUBIM to connect it to the SIC-NS API that MOP handles on their side.  So basically what they do is take charge of the administration and management of the BIM side of projects.

After this Tao shared his perspective on what we aim to, he mentioned the Service Levels we have to comply with and which serves as success index, these are sediment accumulation, filtration, and watering index (the water we deliver for watering crops). He mentioned about IoT devices and how he thinks we could use the BIM models to collect this information and store it so we can use it later from the model to run simulations, generate reports, communicate with others and actually exploit the bim model for the management of infrastructure. He even envision opening and closing valves or mechanisms through the BIM model.

From the talk I got a clear immpression that ProBIM doesn't really develop tools but they pass the requests and developing needs to the YOUBIM team. By the way, YOUBIM is paid software,  SaaS, and ProBIM doesn't come with YOUBIM, we have to hire YOUBIM separately and then ProBIM integrates the tool to how they have operated with it in other contracts. Of course they can customize, but their base, or constraint is what YOUBIM allows. My impression is that this team rather than a R+D team is a process management team according to what they understand from the BALI (Bases de Licitación aka bidding contract). ProBIM did mention that not many contracts have arrived to the data management part and that they have been learning as they advance too, which is right as the chilean market is still trapped in the construction process rather than moving on to data management, mainly, because the industry doesn't know the full potential of BIM.

Tao mentioned again that he aims to reduce operation costs, do preemptive maintenance operations, follow the operation and maintenance from BIM models (or platforms that connect to the model), control and generate reports around CO2 emissions, electrical consumptions, water intake to the dam, water outake from the dam, etc.  

Probim did mention that they have knowledge of several MOP contracts working with YOUBIM and so they named them. But this tells me they don't develope on their own and mostly just integrate tools that are lying around, on the opposite, my view is that there ir to move away from these SaaS tools, as they may disappear in the future and sometimes "marrying" this companies is for worse than for good. For example, we could develope our own model visualizer and integrator as I did with the prototype https://ifcviewer.onbim.cl/ manuals and API endpoints are clear enough on MOP part, what is lacking is custoim software that can connect to MOP and integrate the information they need. I am not aware if you can read PDFs Hermes, confirm to me and I can send you the manual that describes how our system has to connect to MOP so we can
maybe start the update of our IFC model viewer and start creating the app to connect to MOP.

Anyways, the meeting ended with us (the SC) sending proBIM our BALI so they get knowledge of what is requested from MOP to us as SC. We should also define the service levels we want to operate together with the BIM models during operation and analyze from here how we move. Meeting ended abruptly, as usual with this company (the SC) so I told Tao that we should have a debrief meeting. I did this because usually these meetings end up here and they keep us out of the loop, by they I mean management, in this case SCCM, but as this is a BIM topic and I am the BIM at the SC, my perspective is that we should have a debrief meeting after the proBIM meeting to assess if we all see the same thing or if we have diffrerent opinions. Also to discuss how we see the landscape and how we should proceed. Finally, having a debrief meeting can help us bettrer define where this idea may go and we can route it better. Tao agreed on continue the discussion of this, so we set up a new meeting for 3 pm this afternoon.

## PROBIM Meeting — BIM for Dam Exploitation / Data Management

## PROBIM Presentation — What They Do

### Profile
- **UBIM (BIM Unit)** that takes responsibility in front of MOP for the processes the concessionary carries out with their own personnel
- Essentially an **outsourcing BIM services company**
- ⚠️ Note: DGC (Dirección General de Concesiones) removed this working model around 2020 because some companies misused it to leverage projects. O'car's contract and SC **explicitly require the UBIM to be part of the SC**, not outsourced.

### Workflow
1. Take BIM models
2. Update them during construction stage
3. Prepare for operation stage — add information
4. Integrate into **YOUBIM** (SaaS platform)
5. Connect to **SIC-NS API** (MOP's system on their side)

### YOUBIM
- **Paid SaaS software** — not included with ProBIM
- SC must hire YOUBIM separately
- ProBIM then integrates it based on their experience with other MOP contracts
- Customization is possible but constrained by what YOUBIM allows
- ProBIM passes development needs to the YOUBIM team

### O'car's Impression of ProBIM
- More of a **process management team** than an R+D team
- They work within what YOUBIM permits
- Not many contracts have reached the data management phase — they are learning as they go
- Chilean market is still trapped in the **construction process** rather than moving to data management, mainly because the industry doesn't know the full potential of BIM

1. **Sediment accumulation** in the dam
2. **Filtration**
3. **Watering index** — water delivered for crop irrigation (tasa de riego)

- **Collect information** via IoT devices and store in BIM models
- Use models for **simulations, reports, and infrastructure management**
- Open/close **valves and mechanisms** through the BIM model interface
- **Reduce operation costs**
- **Preemptive maintenance** operations
- Follow **O&M from BIM models** or connected platforms
- Control and generate reports: **CO2 emissions, electrical consumption, water intake, water outflow**

- Instead, he advocates **building custom software** that can connect to MOP directly
- Already has a working **IFC viewer prototype**: https://ifcviewer.onbim.cl/
  - Manuals and API endpoints are clear from MOP's side
  - What's missing: **custom software** that connects to MOP and integrates the required information
- O'car's view: rather than marrying SaaS companies (which can be more harmful than good long-term), build our own integration layer

2. **SC to define service levels** — decide what service levels we want to operate with BIM models during operation

---

## O'car's Open Questions / Concerns

- Is ProBIM the right partner for where Tao wants to go?
- Is YOUBIM the right platform, or should SC build its own?
- How does the custom IFC viewer connect to MOP's SIC-NS API?

inventario, y los tipos de problemas que afectarán a cada indicador deben ser adecuadamente abordados
desde la más temprana etapa de su diseño.
El presente Manual otorga los lineamientos técnicos para que los desarrolladores puedan generar el o los
puntos de comunicación de servicios (web service) con SICE de los datos más frecuentemente utilizados
basados en los desarrollos de SIC-NS anteriores. No se pretende abarcar todas las posibilidades y existen
flexibilidad en el tratamiento de cada caso en forma independiente debido a las singularidades que se puedan
presentar.
Cómo parte de la evolución de las BALI se incorporó a los procesos, el BIM, el cual dependiendo de cada
contrato tiene diferentes usos, no obstante, la tendencia es a transformarse en la fuente de datos que alimenta
al resto de las aplicaciones, por lo cual es fundamental que esté alineado con las estructuras de datos que más
adelante necesitará SIC-NS. De acuerdo al Ord. 139 de fecha 25/11/2022, “SICE no se vincula ni intercambia
información directa con los sistemas BIM” no obstante, por lo ya mencionado, el desarrollo de los Sistemas

{"output": "# Simple IFC Viewer

A lightweight, web-based IFC model viewer that allows you to view and interact with IFC (Industry Foundation Classes) models directly in your browser. Built with [@thatopen/components](https://github.com/ThatOpen/engine_components), it provides an easy and seamless viewer for construction and BIM professionals.

## Features

- **Import/Export**
  - Load IFC files directly in your browser
  - Import fragment files (.frag)
  - Export fragment files for faster loading
  - Dispose/reset scene memory

- **Selection & Visibility**
  - Select and highlight model elements
  - Toggle element visibility
  - Isolate selected elements
  - Show/hide all elements

- **Properties & Information**
  - View detailed element properties
  - Access model classification system
  - Search and filter properties
  - Real-time property updates

- **Viewer Settings**
  - Configure viewer appearance
  - Adjust background color
  - Toggle grid visibility
  - Customize viewing experience

## Project Structure

```
ifcBrowser/
├── index.html          # Main entry point
├── vite.config.js      # Vite configuration
├── tsconfig.json       # TypeScript configuration
├── src/
│   ├── viewer/         # TypeScript files for the IFC viewer
│   │   └── index.ts    # Main viewer implementation
│   └── styles/         # CSS styles
│       └── main.css    # Main stylesheet
```

## Getting Started

### Using the built version

1. Download the files inside the `dist` folder.
2. Paste the files into your server.
3. Open the `index.html` file in your browser.
4. Click on the "Import IFC" button to load an IFC model.

### Developing Locally

#### Prerequisites
- Node.js (Latest LTS version recommended)
- npm or yarn package manager

#### Installation

1. Clone the repository:
   ```bash
   git clone https://github.com/OcarCampos/ifcBrowser.git
   cd ifcBrowser
   ```

2. Install dependencies:
   ```bash
   npm install
   ```

3. Start the development server:
   ```bash
   npm run dev
   ```", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}

{"content": "# 20260406-Probim-Dam-DataManagement.md

## PROBIM Meeting — BIM for Dam Exploitation / Data Management

**Date:** Monday April 6, 2026 — 11:00 AM (CLT)
**Calendar:** Reuniones UBIM CHEC
**Duration:** ~1 hour 10 minutes
**Attendees:**
- PROBIM: Julio, Angela
- SC: Tao Liu (CEO), Sergio (SCCM), René, O'car (BIM Manager, Head of Unidad BIM SC)
**Status:** Post-meeting. Debrief scheduled 3pm same day with Tao.
**Prepared by:** Hermes (Sysadmin)

---

## PROBIM Presentation — What They Do

### Profile
- **UBIM (BIM Unit)** that takes responsibility in front of MOP for the processes the concessionary carries out with their own personnel
- Essentially an **outsourcing BIM services company**
- ⚠️ Note: DGC (Dirección General de Concesiones) removed this working model around 2020 because some companies misused it to leverage projects. O'car's contract and SC **explicitly require the UBIM to be part of the SC**, not outsourced.

### Workflow
1. Take BIM models
2. Update them during construction stage
3. Prepare for operation stage — add information
4. Integrate into **YOUBIM** (SaaS platform)
5. Connect to **SIC-NS API** (MOP's system on their side)
6. Administration and management of the BIM side of projects

### YOUBIM
- **Paid SaaS software** — not included with ProBIM
- SC must hire YOUBIM separately
- ProBIM then integrates it based on their experience with other MOP contracts
- Customization is possible but constrained by what YOUBIM allows
- ProBIM passes development needs to the YOUBIM team

### O'car's Impression of ProBIM
- More of a **process management team** than an R+D team
- They work within what YOUBIM permits
- Not many contracts have reached the data management phase — they are learning as they go
- Chilean market is still trapped in the **construction process** rather than moving to data management, mainly because the industry doesn't know the full potential of BIM

---

## Tao Liu's Vision (discussed in meeting)

### Service Levels / Success Indexes (BALI)
1. **Sediment accumulation** in the dam
2. **Filtration**
3. **Watering index** — water delivered for crop irrigation (tasa de riego)

### Tao's Goals for BIM + IoT
- **Collect information** via IoT devices and store in BIM models
- Use models for **simulations, reports, and infrastructure management**
- Open/close **valves and mechanisms** through the BIM model interface
- **Reduce operation costs**
- **Preemptive maintenance** operations
- Follow **O&M from BIM models** or connected platforms
- Control and generate reports: **CO2 emissions, electrical consumption, water intake, water outflow**

---

## MOP SIC-NS Integration Manual — Key Findings

**Document:** Manual Integración SICE - SIC-NS V2 2.0.1 (DGC MOP, UCTO — División de Operaciones)
**Reference:** `/mnt/data/Developing/hermes-workspace/work/Library/Manual Integración SICE - SIC-NS V2 2.0.1.pdf`

### Critical Finding: SICE Does NOT Connect Directly to BIM

> *"De acuerdo al Ord. 139 de fecha 25/11/2022, 'SICE no se vincula ni intercambia información directa con los sistemas BIM'"*
>
> *"SICE no se vincula ni intercambia información directa con los sistemas BIM, sino que se vincula exclusivamente con el SIC NS de cada contrato concesionado."*

The actual integration chain is:
```
BIM System → Data Management Platform → SIC-NS → SICE (MOP)
```

This means:
- YOUBIM or any BIM platform does NOT connect directly to SICE
- The bridge is the **SIC-NS** (Sistema Informático de Constatación de Nivel de Servicio)
- BIM → SIC-NS is the actual integration point we need to solve

### The Three-Stage BIM-SIC-NS Interface

The connection between BIM and SIC-NS is done via **standardized Excel templates** in 3 stages:

1. **Macro Ubicaciones** — Define contract sectors/areas (approved by IF + DGC MOP)
2. **Tipos de Elementos** — Define inventory element types (approved by IF + DGC MOP)
3. **Inventario Georreferenciado** — Georeferenced inventory elements (filed via Reporte 55)

### Contractual BIM Deliverables

| Deliverable | Timing |
|---|---|
| **Prototipo BIM** | 90–180 days before PSP (approved by Inspector Fiscal, not DGC MOP) |
| **SIC-NS** | 90–180 days before PSP |
| **Modelos BIM As Built** | 90–180 days after PSP |

### What This Means for Our Project

- The integration between our BIM system and MOP's SIC-NS is defined by **standardized Excel templates** — not API calls
- The Prototipo BIM must contain all technical specifications for the BIM System, approved by IF
- YOUBIM or any SaaS is just a tool — the actual contract requirement is the **process and the Excel outputs**
- Our custom IFC viewer at https://ifcviewer.onbim.cl could potentially generate or feed the required Excel inventories
- The manual is explicit: **the SC's BIM Unit must coordinate with UCTO (DGC MOP) from the earliest stages** to align macro ubicaciones, hectómetros, and inventory structure

### Key Manual Quotes for Reference

> *"La tendencia es a [que BIM] se transforme en la fuente de datos que alimenta al resto de las aplicaciones, por lo cual es fundamental que esté alineado con las estructuras de datos que más adelante necesitará SIC-NS."*

> *"El entregable o resultado de la vinculación de nivel de información entre el Sistema BIM con el SIC NS son planillas de cálculo en formato Excel."*

---

## O'car's Position

- O'car believes the SC should **move away from SaaS dependency** — these tools may disappear or become obsolete
- Instead, he advocates **building custom software** that can connect to MOP directly
- Already has a working **IFC viewer prototype**: https://ifcviewer.onbim.cl/
  - Manuals and API endpoints are clear from MOP's side
  - What's missing: **custom software** that connects to MOP and integrates the required information
- O'car's view: rather than marrying SaaS companies (which can be more harmful than good long-term), build our own integration layer
- The manual confirms the integration is via **Excel templates** — which is within reach of a custom tool

---

## Agreements / Outcomes from Meeting

1. **SC to send ProBIM the BALI** — so they understand what MOP requires from the SC
2. **SC to define service levels** — decide what service levels we want to operate with BIM models during operation
3. **ProBIM to analyze** how to move forward given SC's context
4. **Debrief meeting scheduled:** 3pm today with Tao, Sergio, René — to align internal SC view before moving further with ProBIM

---

## Next Steps

| # | Action | Owner | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Send ProBIM the BALI (Bases de Licitación) | O'car / Tao | 🔴 Pending |
| 2 | Define service levels for BIM operational management | SC (Tao + O'car) | 🔴 Pending — debrief 3pm |
| 3 | Internal SC debrief with Tao | Tao, Sergio, René, O'car | 🟡 Today 3pm |
| 4 | Assess YOUBIM vs custom development path | O'car | 🟡 Pending |
| 5 | Review MOP IFC/API manual for custom integration | O'car + Hermes | ✅ Analyzed |
| 6 | Follow up on ProBIM's analysis | O'car | 🟡 Pending |
| 7 | Schedule UCTO coordination meeting (per manual requirement) | O'car | 🟡 Pending |
| 8 | Propose Prototipo BIM scope for dam context | SC + ProBIM | 🟡 Pending |

---

## O'car's Open Questions / Concerns

- Is ProBIM the right partner for where Tao wants to go?
- Is YOUBIM the right platform, or should SC build its own?
- How does the custom IFC viewer connect to MOP's SIC-NS API?
- What exactly are the contractual requirements for BIM data delivery at operation stage?
- Should SC's UBIM be building custom tools rather than relying on SaaS integrators?
- Has SC already coordinated with UCTO (DGC MOP) on macro ubicaciones and inventory structure? (Manual requires this from early stages)

---
*Last updated: 2026-04-06*", "path": "/mnt/data/Developing/hermes-workspace/work/20260406-probim-dam-data-management.md"}

File updated. Here's my read on your position and how I'd frame it at 3pm:

---

**My thoughts on what you said:**

You're right on every point. The manual actually **reinforces your instinct** — SICE doesn't connect to BIM directly. The bridge is SIC-NS, and that bridge is crossed via **standardized Excel templates** (Macro Ubicaciones → Tipos de Elementos → Inventario Georreferenciado). That's not a complex API — it's structured spreadsheet work. Your IFC viewer could own that workflow end-to-end, giving SC full control.

YOUBIM as a platform isn't the requirement — it's one possible tool. The real contractual deliverable is the **Prototipo BIM** and the **As-Built models**, plus the Excel-based inventory integration with SIC-NS. ProBIM knowing YOUBIM well is useful but doesn't mean YOUBIM is the only path.

**On how to phrase it at 3pm — diplomatic but clear:**

Instead of saying *"ProBIM is not R+D,"* try:
> *"ProBIM has shown solid experience in executing the process with YOUBIM in other contracts — that's valuable. Our situation is different: we need to define exactly what we want from the BIM system during operation before we choose tools or partners. We should start from the contract requirements, not from the tool. Once we know what we need, we can evaluate whether YOUBIM, a custom solution, or a combination is the right fit for us."*

This is firm without being attacking, and puts the focus back on **process clarity** — which is the actual blocker right now.

**The one thing I'd flag at the meeting that I haven't heard addressed yet:**

Has the SC already coordinated with **UCTO (DGC MOP)** on macro ubicaciones and inventory structure? The manual is explicit that this coordination must happen from the earliest stages. If we haven't done this, we're missing a foundational step that ProBIM should be facilitating — not just handing us a YOUBIM demo.

---

Good position. Let me know how the 3pm goes. 📋

function.name: browser_back
function.description: Navigate back to the previous page in browser history. Requires browser_navigate to be called first.

function.name: browser_click
function.description: Click on an element identified by its ref ID from the snapshot (e.g., '@e5'). The ref IDs are shown in square brackets in the snapshot output. Requires browser_navigate and browser_snapshot to be called first.

ref.description: The element reference from the snapshot (e.g., '@e5', '@e12')

function.name: browser_close
function.description: Close the browser session and release resources. Call this when done with browser tasks to free up Browserbase session quota.

function.name: browser_console
function.description: Get browser console output and JavaScript errors from the current page. Returns console.log/warn/error/info messages and uncaught JS exceptions. Use this to detect silent JavaScript errors, failed API calls, and application warnings. Requires browser_navigate to be called first. When 'expression' is provided, evaluates JavaScript in the page context and returns the result — use this for DOM inspection, reading page state, or extracting data programmatically.

clear.description: If true, clear the message buffers after reading

expression.description: JavaScript expression to evaluate in the page context. Runs in the browser like DevTools console — full access to DOM, window, document. Return values are serialized to JSON. Example: 'document.title' or 'document.querySelectorAll("a").length'

function.name: browser_get_images
function.description: Get a list of all images on the current page with their URLs and alt text. Useful for finding images to analyze with the vision tool. Requires browser_navigate to be called first.

function.name: browser_navigate
function.description: Navigate to a URL in the browser. Initializes the session and loads the page. Must be called before other browser tools. Use browser tools when you need to interact with a page (click, fill forms, dynamic content).

function.name: browser_press
function.description: Press a keyboard key. Useful for submitting forms (Enter), navigating (Tab), or keyboard shortcuts. Requires browser_navigate to be called first.

function.name: browser_scroll
function.description: Scroll the page in a direction. Use this to reveal more content that may be below or above the current viewport. Requires browser_navigate to be called first.

function.name: browser_snapshot
function.description: Get a text-based snapshot of the current page's accessibility tree. Returns interactive elements with ref IDs (like @e1, @e2) for browser_click and browser_type. full=false (default): compact view with interactive elements. full=true: complete page content. Snapshots over 8000 chars are truncated or LLM-summarized. Requires browser_navigate first.

full.description: If true, returns complete page content. If false (default), returns compact view with interactive elements only.

function.name: browser_type
function.description: Type text into an input field identified by its ref ID. Clears the field first, then types the new text. Requires browser_navigate and browser_snapshot to be called first.

ref.description: The element reference from the snapshot (e.g., '@e3')

text.description: The text to type into the field

function.name: browser_vision
function.description: Take a screenshot of the current page and analyze it with vision AI. Use this when you need to visually understand what's on the page - especially useful for CAPTCHAs, visual verification challenges, complex layouts, or when the text snapshot doesn't capture important visual information. Returns both the AI analysis and a screenshot_path that you can share with the user by including MEDIA:<screenshot_path> in your response. Requires browser_navigate to be called first.

question.description: What you want to know about the page visually. Be specific about what you're looking for.

annotate.description: If true, overlay numbered [N] labels on interactive elements. Each [N] maps to ref @eN for subsequent browser commands. Useful for QA and spatial reasoning about page layout.

function.name: clarify
function.description:
Ask the user a question when you need clarification, feedback, or a decision before proceeding. Supports two modes:

1. **Multiple choice** — provide up to 4 choices. The user picks one or types their own answer via a 5th 'Other' option.
2. **Open-ended** — omit choices entirely. The user types a free-form response.

Use this tool when:
- The task is ambiguous and you need the user to choose an approach
- You want post-task feedback ('How did that work out?')
- You want to offer to save a skill or update memory
- A decision has meaningful trade-offs the user should weigh in on

Do NOT use this tool for simple yes/no confirmation of dangerous commands (the terminal tool handles that). Prefer making a reasonable default choice yourself when the decision is low-stakes.

question.description: The question to present to the user.

choices.description: Up to 4 answer choices. Omit this parameter entirely to ask an open-ended question. When provided, the UI automatically appends an 'Other (type your answer)' option.

function.name: cronjob
function.description:
Manage scheduled cron jobs with a single compressed tool.

Use action='create' to schedule a new job from a prompt or one or more skills.
Use action='list' to inspect jobs.
Use action='update', 'pause', 'resume', 'remove', or 'run' to manage an existing job.

Jobs run in a fresh session with no current-chat context, so prompts must be self-contained.
If skill or skills are provided on create, the future cron run loads those skills in order, then follows the prompt as the task instruction.
On update, passing skills=[] clears attached skills.

If script is provided on create, the referenced Python script runs before each agent turn.
Its stdout is injected into the prompt as context. Use this for data collection and change
detection — the script handles gathering data, the agent analyzes and reports.
On update, pass script="" to clear an attached script.

NOTE: The agent's final response is auto-delivered to the target. Put the primary
user-facing content in the final response. Cron jobs run autonomously with no user
present — they cannot ask questions or request clarification.

Important safety rule: cron-run sessions should not recursively schedule more cron jobs.

action.description: One of: create, list, update, pause, resume, remove, run

prompt.description: For create: the full self-contained prompt. If skill or skills are also provided, this becomes the task instruction paired with those skills.

repeat.description: Optional repeat count. Omit for defaults (once for one-shot, forever for recurring).

deliver.description: Delivery target: origin, local, telegram, discord, slack, whatsapp, signal, matrix, mattermost, homeassistant, dingtalk, feishu, wecom, email, sms, or platform:chat_id or platform:chat_id:thread_id for Telegram topics. Examples: 'origin', 'local', 'telegram', 'telegram:-1001234567890:17585', 'discord:#engineering'

model.description: Optional per-job model override used when the cron job runs

provider.description: Optional per-job provider override used when resolving runtime credentials

base_url.description: Optional per-job base URL override paired with provider/model routing

skill.description: Optional single skill name to load before executing the cron prompt

skills.description: Optional ordered list of skills to load before executing the cron prompt. On update, pass an empty array to clear attached skills.

script.description: Optional path to a Python script that runs before each cron job execution. Its stdout is injected into the prompt as context. Use for data collection and change detection. Relative paths resolve under ~/.hermes/scripts/. On update, pass empty string to clear.

function.name: delegate_task
function.description:
Spawn one or more subagents to work on tasks in isolated contexts. Each subagent gets its own conversation, terminal session, and toolset. Only the final summary is returned -- intermediate tool results never enter your context window.

TWO MODES (one of 'goal' or 'tasks' is required):
1. Single task: provide 'goal' (+ optional context, toolsets)
2. Batch (parallel): provide 'tasks' array with up to 3 items. All run concurrently and results are returned together.

WHEN TO USE delegate_task:
- Reasoning-heavy subtasks (debugging, code review, research synthesis)
- Tasks that would flood your context with intermediate data
- Parallel independent workstreams (research A and B simultaneously)

WHEN NOT TO USE (use these instead):
- Mechanical multi-step work with no reasoning needed -> use execute_code
- Single tool call -> just call the tool directly
- Tasks needing user interaction -> subagents cannot use clarify

IMPORTANT:
- Subagents have NO memory of your conversation. Pass all relevant info (file paths, error messages, constraints) via the 'context' field.
- Subagents CANNOT call: delegate_task, clarify, memory, send_message, execute_code.
- Each subagent gets its own terminal session (separate working directory and state).
- Results are always returned as an array, one entry per task.

goal.description: What the subagent should accomplish. Be specific and self-contained -- the subagent knows nothing about your conversation history.

context.description: Background information the subagent needs: file paths, error messages, project structure, constraints. The more specific you are, the better the subagent performs.

toolsets.description: Toolsets to enable for this subagent. Default: inherits your enabled toolsets. Common patterns: ['terminal', 'file'] for code work, ['web'] for research, ['terminal', 'file', 'web'] for full-stack tasks.
Toolsets for this specific task. Use 'web' for network access, 'terminal' for shell.

acp_command.description: Per-task ACP command override (e.g. 'claude'). Overrides the top-level acp_command for this task only.

tasks.description: Batch mode: up to 3 tasks to run in parallel. Each gets its own subagent with isolated context and terminal session. When provided, top-level goal/context/toolsets are ignored.

max_iterations.description: Max tool-calling turns per subagent (default: 50). Only set lower for simple tasks.

acp_command.description: Override ACP command for child agents (e.g. 'claude', 'copilot'). When set, children use ACP subprocess transport instead of inheriting the parent's transport. Enables spawning Claude Code (claude --acp --stdio) or other ACP-capable agents from any parent, including Discord/Telegram/CLI.

acp_args.description: Arguments for the ACP command (default: ['--acp', '--stdio']). Only used when acp_command is set. Example: ['--acp', '--stdio', '--model', 'claude-opus-4-6']

function.name: execute_code
function.description:
Run a Python script that can call Hermes tools programmatically. Use this when you need 3+ tool calls with processing logic between them, need to filter/reduce large tool outputs before they enter your context, need conditional branching (if X then Y else Z), or need to loop (fetch N pages, process N files, retry on failure).

Use normal tool calls instead when: single tool call with no processing, you need to see the full result and apply complex reasoning, or the task requires interactive user input.

Available via `from hermes_tools import ...`:

  read_file(path: str, offset: int = 1, limit: int = 500) -> dict
    Lines are 1-indexed. Returns {"content": "...", "total_lines": N}
  write_file(path: str, content: str) -> dict
    Always overwrites the entire file.
  search_files(pattern: str, target="content", path=".", file_glob=None, limit=50) -> dict
    target: "content" (search inside files) or "files" (find files by name). Returns {"matches": [...]}
  patch(path: str, old_string: str, new_string: str, replace_all: bool = False) -> dict
    Replaces old_string with new_string in the file.
  terminal(command: str, timeout=None, workdir=None) -> dict
    Foreground only (no background/pty). Returns {"output": "...", "exit_code": N}

Limits: 5-minute timeout, 50KB stdout cap, max 50 tool calls per script. terminal() is foreground-only (no background or pty). If the session uses a cloud sandbox backend, treat it as resumable task state rather than a durable always-on machine.

Print your final result to stdout. Use Python stdlib (json, re, math, csv, datetime, collections, etc.) for processing between tool calls.

Also available (no import needed — built into hermes_tools):
  json_parse(text: str) — json.loads with strict=False; use for terminal() output with control chars
  shell_quote(s: str) — shlex.quote(); use when interpolating dynamic strings into shell commands
  retry(fn, max_attempts=3, delay=2) — retry with exponential backoff for transient failures

code.description: Python code to execute. Import tools with `from hermes_tools import terminal, ...` and print your final result to stdout.

function.name: image_generate
function.description: Generate high-quality images from text prompts using FLUX 2 Pro model with automatic 2x upscaling. Creates detailed, artistic images that are automatically upscaled for hi-rez results. Returns a single upscaled image URL. Display it using markdown: ![description](URL)

prompt.description: The text prompt describing the desired image. Be detailed and descriptive.

aspect_ratio.description: The aspect ratio of the generated image. 'landscape' is 16:9 wide, 'portrait' is 16:9 tall, 'square' is 1:1.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: send_message
function.description:
Send a message to a connected messaging platform, or list available targets.

IMPORTANT: When the user asks to send to a specific channel or person (not just a bare platform name), call send_message(action='list') FIRST to see available targets, then send to the correct one.
If the user just says a platform name like 'send to telegram', send directly to the home channel without listing first.

action.description: Action to perform. 'send' (default) sends a message. 'list' returns all available channels/contacts across connected platforms.

target.description: Delivery target. Format: 'platform' (uses home channel), 'platform:#channel-name', 'platform:chat_id', or Telegram topic 'telegram:chat_id:thread_id'. Examples: 'telegram', 'telegram:-1001234567890:17585', 'discord:#bot-home', 'slack:#engineering', 'signal:+15551234567'

function.name: session_search
function.description:
Search your long-term memory of past conversations, or browse recent sessions. This is your recall -- every past session is searchable, and this tool summarizes what happened.

TWO MODES:
1. Recent sessions (no query): Call with no arguments to see what was worked on recently. Returns titles, previews, and timestamps. Zero LLM cost, instant. Start here when the user asks what were we working on or what did we do recently.
2. Keyword search (with query): Search for specific topics across all past sessions. Returns LLM-generated summaries of matching sessions.

USE THIS PROACTIVELY when:
- The user says 'we did this before', 'remember when', 'last time', 'as I mentioned'
- The user asks about a topic you worked on before but don't have in current context
- The user references a project, person, or concept that seems familiar but isn't in memory
- You want to check if you've solved a similar problem before
- The user asks 'what did we do about X?' or 'how did we fix Y?'

Don't hesitate to search when it is actually cross-session -- it's fast and cheap. Better to search and confirm than to guess or ask the user to repeat themselves.

Search syntax: keywords joined with OR for broad recall (elevenlabs OR baseten OR funding), phrases for exact match ("docker networking"), boolean (python NOT java), prefix (deploy*). IMPORTANT: Use OR between keywords for best results — FTS5 defaults to AND which misses sessions that only mention some terms. If a broad OR query returns nothing, try individual keyword searches in parallel. Returns summaries of the top matching sessions.

query.description: Search query — keywords, phrases, or boolean expressions to find in past sessions. Omit this parameter entirely to browse recent sessions instead (returns titles, previews, timestamps with no LLM cost).

role_filter.description: Optional: only search messages from specific roles (comma-separated). E.g. 'user,assistant' to skip tool outputs.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills)

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for everything that finishes.
Background: ONLY for long-running servers, watchers, or processes that never exit. Set background=true to get a session_id, then use process(action="wait") to block until done — it returns instantly on completion, same as foreground. Use process(action="poll") only when you need a progress check without blocking.
Do NOT use background for scripts, builds, or installs — foreground with a generous timeout is always better (fewer tool calls, instant results).
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: ONLY for servers/watchers that never exit. For scripts, builds, installs — use foreground with timeout instead (it returns instantly when done).

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

function.name: text_to_speech
function.description: Convert text to speech audio. Returns a MEDIA: path that the platform delivers as a voice message. On Telegram it plays as a voice bubble, on Discord/WhatsApp as an audio attachment. In CLI mode, saves to ~/voice-memos/. Voice and provider are user-configured, not model-selected.

text.description: The text to convert to speech. Keep under 4000 characters.

output_path.description: Optional custom file path to save the audio. Defaults to ~/.hermes/audio_cache/<timestamp>.mp3

function.name: todo
function.description:
Manage your task list for the current session. Use for complex tasks with 3+ steps or when the user provides multiple tasks. Call with no parameters to read the current list.

Writing:
- Provide 'todos' array to create/update items
- merge=false (default): replace the entire list with a fresh plan
- merge=true: update existing items by id, add any new ones

Each item: {id: string, content: string, status: pending|in_progress|completed|cancelled}
List order is priority. Only ONE item in_progress at a time.
Mark items completed immediately when done. If something fails, cancel it and add a revised item.

Always returns the full current list.

todos.description: Task items to write. Omit to read current list.

merge.description: true: update existing items by id, add new ones. false (default): replace the entire list.

function.name: vision_analyze
function.description: Analyze images using AI vision. Provides a comprehensive description and answers a specific question about the image content.

image_url.description: Image URL (http/https) or local file path to analyze.

question.description: Your specific question or request about the image to resolve. The AI will automatically provide a complete image description AND answer your specific question.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file
